content:
# Genesis Chapter 7 -- The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 4v–5r | **Source Images:** source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_007_page_001.jpg, GEN_007_page_002.jpg
**Additional Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209) — LXX | **View Vaticanus online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0009

---

**7:1** And YHWH said [וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה] to Noah [לְנֹחַ]: Come [בֹּא], you [אַתָּה] and all your household [וְכָל-בֵּיתְךָ], into the ark [אֶל-הַתֵּבָה], for you [כִּי-אֹתְךָ] I have seen [רָאִיתִי] righteous [צַדִּיק] before me [לְפָנַי] in this generation [בַּדּוֹר הַזֶּה].

**7:2** From all the clean animals [מִכֹּל הַבְּהֵמָה הַטְּהוֹרָה], take for yourself [תִּקַּח-לְךָ] seven and seven [שִׁבְעָה שִׁבְעָה], male and his female [אִישׁ וְאִשְׁתּוֹ]; and from the animals that are not clean [וּמִן-הַבְּהֵמָה אֲשֶׁר לֹא טְהֹרָה הִוא], two [שְׁנַיִם], male and his female [אִישׁ וְאִשְׁתּוֹ].

**7:3** Also from the flying-things of the heavens [גַּם מֵעוֹף הַשָּׁמַיִם] seven and seven [שִׁבְעָה שִׁבְעָה], male and female [זָכָר וּנְקֵבָה], to keep alive seed [לְחַיּוֹת זֶרַע] upon the face of all the earth [עַל-פְּנֵי כָל-הָאָרֶץ].

**7:4** For in yet seven days [כִּי לְיָמִים עוֹד שִׁבְעָה] I am causing rain to fall [אָנֹכִי מַמְטִיר] upon the earth [עַל-הָאָרֶץ] forty days [אַרְבָּעִים יוֹם] and forty nights [וְאַרְבָּעִים לָיְלָה], and I will wipe away [וּמָחִיתִי] every living thing [אֶת-כָּל-הַיְקוּם] that I have made [אֲשֶׁר עָשִׂיתִי] from upon the face of the ground [מֵעַל פְּנֵי הָאֲדָמָה].

**7:5** And Noah did [וַיַּעַשׂ נֹחַ] according to all [כְּכֹל] that YHWH commanded him [אֲשֶׁר-צִוָּהוּ יְהוָה].

**7:6** And Noah was a son of six hundred years [וְנֹחַ בֶּן-שֵׁשׁ מֵאוֹת שָׁנָה], and the flood [וְהַמַּבּוּל] was waters [הָיָה מַיִם] upon the earth [עַל-הָאָרֶץ].

**7:7** And Noah entered [וַיָּבֹא נֹחַ], and his sons [וּבָנָיו], and his wife [וְאִשְׁתּוֹ], and his sons' wives with him [וּנְשֵׁי-בָנָיו אִתּוֹ], into the ark [אֶל-הַתֵּבָה], from before the waters of the flood [מִפְּנֵי מֵי הַמַּבּוּל].

**7:8** From the clean animals [מִן-הַבְּהֵמָה הַטְּהוֹרָה] and from the animals that are not clean [וּמִן-הַבְּהֵמָה אֲשֶׁר אֵינֶנָּה טְהֹרָה], and from the flying-things [וּמִן-הָעוֹף], and from every creeping thing upon the ground [וְכֹל אֲשֶׁר-רֹמֵשׂ עַל-הָאֲדָמָה] —

**7:9** two and two [שְׁנַיִם שְׁנַיִם] they came [בָּאוּ] to Noah [אֶל-נֹחַ] into the ark [אֶל-הַתֵּבָה], male and female [זָכָר וּנְקֵבָה], as God had commanded Noah [כַּאֲשֶׁר צִוָּה אֱלֹהִים אֶת-נֹחַ].

**7:10** And it came to pass [וַיְהִי] after the seven days [לְשִׁבְעַת הַיָּמִים] that the waters of the flood [וּמֵי הַמַּבּוּל] were upon the earth [הָיוּ עַל-הָאָרֶץ].

**7:11** In the six hundredth year of Noah's life [בִּשְׁנַת שֵׁשׁ-מֵאוֹת שָׁנָה לְחַיֵּי-נֹחַ], in the second month [בַּחֹדֶשׁ הַשֵּׁנִי], on the seventeenth day of the month [בְּשִׁבְעָה-עָשָׂר יוֹם לַחֹדֶשׁ] — on this day [בַּיּוֹם הַזֶּה] all the fountains of the great deep burst open [נִבְקְעוּ כָּל-מַעְיְנֹת תְּהוֹם רַבָּה], and the windows of the heavens were opened [וַאֲרֻבֹּת הַשָּׁמַיִם נִפְתָּחוּ].

**7:12** And the rain [וַיְהִי הַגֶּשֶׁם] was upon the earth [עַל-הָאָרֶץ] forty days [אַרְבָּעִים יוֹם] and forty nights [וְאַרְבָּעִים לָיְלָה].

**7:13** On this very day [בְּעֶצֶם הַיּוֹם הַזֶּה] Noah entered [בָּא נֹחַ], and Shem and Ham and Yaphet the sons of Noah [וְשֵׁם-וְחָם וָיֶפֶת בְּנֵי-נֹחַ], and Noah's wife [וְאֵשֶׁת נֹחַ], and the three wives of his sons with them [וּשְׁלֹשֶׁת נְשֵׁי-בָנָיו אִתָּם], into the ark [אֶל-הַתֵּבָה].

**7:14** They [הֵמָּה] and every living thing according to its kind [וְכָל-הַחַיָּה לְמִינָהּ], and every beast according to its kind [וְכָל-הַבְּהֵמָה לְמִינָהּ], and every creeping thing that creeps upon the earth according to its kind [וְכָל-הָרֶמֶשׂ הָרֹמֵשׂ עַל-הָאָרֶץ לְמִינֵהוּ], and every flying-thing according to its kind [וְכָל-הָעוֹף לְמִינֵהוּ], every bird [כֹּל צִפּוֹר], every winged-thing [כָּל-כָּנָף].

**7:15** And they came [וַיָּבֹאוּ] to Noah [אֶל-נֹחַ] into the ark [אֶל-הַתֵּבָה], two and two [שְׁנַיִם שְׁנַיִם] from all the flesh [מִכָּל-הַבָּשָׂר] in which is the breath of life [אֲשֶׁר-בּוֹ רוּחַ חַיִּים].

**7:16** And those who came [וְהַבָּאִים] were male and female from all flesh [זָכָר וּנְקֵבָה מִכָּל-בָּשָׂר], as God had commanded him [כַּאֲשֶׁר צִוָּה אֹתוֹ אֱלֹהִים]; and YHWH shut [וַיִּסְגֹּר יְהוָה] behind him [בַּעֲדוֹ].

**7:17** And the flood [וַיְהִי הַמַּבּוּל] was forty days [אַרְבָּעִים יוֹם] upon the earth [עַל-הָאָרֶץ], and the waters multiplied [וַיִּרְבּוּ הַמַּיִם] and lifted [וַיִּשְׂאוּ] the ark [אֶת-הַתֵּבָה], and it was lifted [וַתָּרָם] above the earth [מֵעַל הָאָרֶץ].

**7:18** And the waters prevailed [וַיִּגְבְּרוּ הַמַּיִם] and multiplied greatly [וַיִּרְבּוּ מְאֹד] upon the earth [עַל-הָאָרֶץ], and the ark went [וַתֵּלֶךְ הַתֵּבָה] upon the face of the waters [עַל-פְּנֵי הַמָּיִם].

**7:19** And the waters prevailed more and more [וְהַמַּיִם גָּבְרוּ מְאֹד מְאֹד] upon the earth [עַל-הָאָרֶץ], and all the high mountains [וַיְכֻסּוּ כָּל-הֶהָרִים הַגְּבֹהִים] under all the heavens [אֲשֶׁר-תַּחַת כָּל-הַשָּׁמָיִם] were covered [הָיוּ תַּחַת הַמָּיִם].

**7:20** Fifteen cubits upward [חֲמֵשׁ עֶשְׂרֵה אַמָּה מִלְמַעְלָה] the waters prevailed [גָּבְרוּ הַמַּיִם], and the mountains were covered [וַיְכֻסּוּ הֶהָרִים].

**7:21** And all flesh expired [וַיִּגְוַע כָּל-בָּשָׂר] that moves [הָרֹמֵשׂ] upon the earth [עַל-הָאָרֶץ] — of flying-things [בָּעוֹף], and of beasts [וּבַבְּהֵמָה], and of living things [וּבַחַיָּה], and of every creeping thing [וּבְכָל-הַשֶּׁרֶץ] that creeps upon the earth [הַשֹּׁרֵץ עַל-הָאָרֶץ], and every adam [וְכָל הָאָדָם].

**7:22** All in whose nostrils was the breath of the spirit of life [כֹּל אֲשֶׁר נִשְׁמַת-רוּחַ חַיִּים בְּאַפָּיו] — from all that was in the dry land [מִכֹּל אֲשֶׁר בֶּחָרָבָה] — died [מֵתוּ].

**7:23** And he wiped away [וַיִּמַח] every living thing [אֶת-כָּל-הַיְקוּם] that was upon the face of the ground [אֲשֶׁר עַל-פְּנֵי הָאֲדָמָה], from adam to beast [מֵאָדָם עַד-בְּהֵמָה], to creeping thing [עַד-רֶמֶשׂ] and to flying-things of the heavens [וְעַד-עוֹף הַשָּׁמָיִם]; and they were wiped away [וַיִּמָּחוּ] from the earth [מִן-הָאָרֶץ]. And only Noah remained [וַיִּשָּׁאֶר אַךְ-נֹחַ], and those who were with him in the ark [וַאֲשֶׁר אִתּוֹ בַּתֵּבָה].

**7:24** And the waters prevailed [וַיִּגְבְּרוּ הַמַּיִם] upon the earth [עַל-הָאָרֶץ] one hundred and fifty days [חֲמִשִּׁים וּמְאַת יוֹם].

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

### On 7:1 — YHWH Speaks (Not Elohim); "Before Me"

The chapter opens with יְהוָה (YHWH), the personal divine name, in direct speech to Noah. This is the divine voice of relationship and covenantal intimacy, not the cosmic authoritative Elohim of 6:13-22. The shift marks the beginning of a YHWH-source sequence (vv. 1-5) that frames the embarkation in personal, relational terms.

The verdict on Noah is precise: צַדִּיק רָאִיתִי אֹתְךָ לְפָנַי — "righteous I have seen you before me." Three elements converge:

1. **צַדִּיק ("righteous"):** The legal-moral term established in 6:9. Here it appears in divine speech as YHWH's own evaluation, not the narrator's assessment. The righteous verdict is spoken to Noah's face.

2. **לְפָנַי ("before me"):** Not "in his generation" (as in 6:9, the narrator's comparative qualifier) but "before me" — the standard of comparison is YHWH's own presence. This is an absolute judgment, not a relative one. The phrase לְפָנַי ("before my face") is an idiom of presence, accountability, and visibility: Noah has been seen standing in the divine court and found righteous.

3. **בַּדּוֹר הַזֶּה ("in this generation"):** This phrase qualifies the righteousness contextually — it is righteousness within the specific generation of judgment. Compare 6:9: בְּדֹרֹתָיו ("in his generations"). Here the singular הַזֶּה ("this") sharpens the contemporaneity: among the people now living, at this moment, Noah alone is righteous.

**The divine name:** The use of YHWH rather than Elohim is characteristic of what classical source criticism identifies as the J (Yahwist) source. The alternation through chs. 6-8 between YHWH and Elohim is one of the primary markers used to identify intertwined sources in the flood narrative. EMET notes this alternation throughout without adjudicating source questions.

**LXX (Vaticanus) 7:1:** εἴσελθε σὺ καὶ πᾶς ὁ οἶκός σου εἰς τὴν κιβωτόν· ὅτι σὲ εἶδον δίκαιον ἐναντίον μου ἐν τῇ γενεᾷ ταύτῃ. The LXX renders לְפָנַי as ἐναντίον μου — "before/facing me" — correctly preserving the sense of divine visibility. The word for ark is κιβωτόν (kibōton), "box/chest/ark," which becomes the standard LXX and NT term (also used for the Ark of the Covenant in Ex. 25, and in 1 Pet. 3:20, Heb. 11:7).

---

### On 7:2 — Clean and Unclean Before Sinai

The distinction between טָהוֹר ("clean") and לֹא טָהוֹר / טָמֵא ("not clean / unclean") animals appears here centuries before the Sinaitic legislation of Leviticus 11 and Deuteronomy 14. This is theologically and narratively significant in multiple directions:

**The asymmetry of the command:** Seven and seven (שִׁבְעָה שִׁבְעָה) of the clean animals; two (שְׁנַיִם) of the unclean. The phrase שִׁבְעָה שִׁבְעָה is a distributive construction: "seven seven" = "seven each" = seven pairs (14 animals). This is confirmed by the parallel with vv. 8-9 where the entry is "two and two" (שְׁנַיִם שְׁנַיִם) — pairs — for all creatures. The greater number of clean animals serves two purposes: (a) surplus for sacrifice after the flood (8:20); (b) food supply after the flood (9:3). The unclean animals come as a minimum pair sufficient for preservation of kind.

**"Male and his female" (אִישׁ וְאִשְׁתּוֹ):** Literally "a man and his woman/wife." The use of human relationship vocabulary (אִישׁ / אִשְׁתּוֹ) for animals is notable — it humanizes the pairing, or more precisely uses the standard Hebrew idiom for matched pairs in an intimate relationship. The same word-pair will appear at the central moment of the flood's reversal.

**The pre-Sinaitic clean/unclean distinction:** The Priestly legislation of Leviticus 11 is presented as a new revelation at Sinai. But the Noah narrative assumes the distinction as already known to Noah. This creates a narrative tension: either (a) the distinction is primordial, embedded in creation itself (and Sinai codifies what was always true), or (b) the narrator is anachronistically projecting later categories onto the early narrative. The text does not resolve this. What the text does establish is that Noah's world already operates with a taxonomy of creatures that will later be formalized. The flood narrative is, among other things, a proto-Sinai: Noah as a new Adam, the ark as a proto-tabernacle, the clean/unclean distinction as proto-Torah. The typological freight is heavy.

**LXX 7:2:** The LXX reads "seven and seven, male and female" (ἑπτὰ ἑπτά, ἄρσεν καὶ θῆλυ) for the clean animals, then "two, male and female" (δύο, ἄρσεν καὶ θῆλυ) for the unclean. The LXX substitutes the impersonal ἄρσεν καὶ θῆλυ ("male and female") for the MT's intimate אִישׁ וְאִשְׁתּוֹ ("a man and his woman"). The gendered pairing language of the Hebrew — implying relational unity — is leveled to a purely biological binary in the Greek.

---

### On 7:4 — הַיְקוּם (Ha-yequm): "The Standing/Living-Thing"

The word יְקוּם (yequm) is rare and semantically rich. It derives from קוּם ("to stand, to rise"), yielding "that which stands/rises" — i.e., living, upright creatures. The word appears four times in the Hebrew Bible (Gen. 7:4, 7:23; Deut. 11:6; Num. 16:30-33), always in contexts of total destruction: all standing things wiped from the ground. It is a collective that encompasses all living things that have upright existence on the earth.

The parallel with מָחָה is exact: in 6:7, YHWH declared אֶמְחֶה ("I will wipe away") the adam; here in 7:4, the object of the wiping is אֶת-כָּל-הַיְקוּם ("every standing-thing I have made") מֵעַל פְּנֵי הָאֲדָמָה ("from upon the face of the ground"). The ground-face receives the wipe. The creation is un-made with the same finality as it was made.

**Forty days and forty nights:** The forty-day/forty-night pattern recurs in the Hebrew Bible as the standard duration of wilderness ordeal:
- Moses on Sinai: 40 days and nights (Ex. 24:18; 34:28; Deut. 9:9)
- Elijah's journey to Horeb: 40 days and nights (1 Kgs. 19:8)
- Israel's wilderness wandering: 40 years
- Jesus' temptation: 40 days and nights (Matt. 4:2; Luke 4:2)

The flood inaugurates the pattern. Forty is not merely a large round number but the scriptural number of transformation through ordeal — a period of total exposure to divine reckoning after which a new reality emerges.

---

### On 7:5 — The Obedience Formula Repeated

וַיַּעַשׂ נֹחַ כְּכֹל אֲשֶׁר-צִוָּהוּ יְהוָה.

"And Noah did according to all that YHWH commanded him."

This is a condensed version of the closing formula of ch. 6 (כְּכֹל אֲשֶׁר צִוָּה אֹתוֹ אֱלֹהִים כֵּן עָשָׂה). Note the divine name: here it is יְהוָה (YHWH), consistent with the YHWH-strand of vv. 1-5. The obedience formula anchors the embarkation narrative and closes the YHWH speech-unit before the narrative shifts back to Elohim in v. 9.

---

### On 7:6 — Noah's Age and the Flood Chronology

Noah is בֶּן-שֵׁשׁ מֵאוֹת שָׁנָה — "a son of six hundred years" — at the time of the flood. The flood date (7:11: second month, seventeenth day of Noah's 600th year) is the first specific calendar date in the Bible, marking the flood as a dateable historical event within the patriarchal chronology. The calendar here is likely a 360-day year (12 months × 30 days), consistent with the flood's arithmetic: the flood rains last 40 days (7:12); the waters prevail 150 days (7:24 / 8:3); the ark rests on Ararat on the 17th day of the 7th month (8:4) — exactly 150 days after the 17th day of the 2nd month; the waters recede through specific dates until the earth dries and Noah exits on the 27th day of the 2nd month of Noah's 601st year (8:14) — a period of exactly 1 year and 10 days (or in a solar year, 365 days).

The flood chronology is thus a precision calendar embedded in the narrative — the most detailed chronological account in the primordial history.

---

### On 7:11 — De-Creation Cosmology: The Fountains and the Windows

בַּיּוֹם הַזֶּה נִבְקְעוּ כָּל-מַעְיְנֹת תְּהוֹם רַבָּה וַאֲרֻבֹּת הַשָּׁמַיִם נִפְתָּחוּ.

"On this day all the fountains of the great deep burst open, and the windows of the heavens were opened."

This verse is the cosmological hinge of the flood narrative. Two primordial water-sources are simultaneously released:

1. **מַעְיְנֹת תְּהוֹם רַבָּה — "Fountains of the great deep":** תְּהוֹם (tehom, "the deep") is the primordial pre-creation waters of Gen. 1:2: וְחֹשֶׁךְ עַל-פְּנֵי תְהוֹם — "and darkness was upon the face of the deep." The תְּהוֹם is the watery chaos that preceded creation, over which the spirit of God hovered. The subterranean waters — the deep below the earth — are now released through their מַעְיְנֹת ("fountains/springs/eyes"). The verb נִבְקְעוּ (from בָּקַע, "to split, cleave, burst") is violent: the earth's floor is split open.

2. **אֲרֻבֹּת הַשָּׁמַיִם — "Windows/floodgates of the heavens":** The אֲרֻבָּה (arubba) is an opening or latticed window. In Gen. 1:6-7, the raqia (firmament) separated the waters above from the waters below. The waters above the firmament are now released through their windows. The cosmological architecture of creation — the firmament as a dividing structure — is breached.

The flood is therefore not merely heavy rainfall. It is the reversal of Genesis 1:6-9:
- In creation: the waters above and below the firmament were separated; the dry land appeared.
- In the flood: the waters above (released through the windows of heaven) and the waters below (released through the fountains of the deep) reunite. The dry land disappears.

The flood is de-creation: a return to תֹהוּ וָבֹהוּ ("formlessness and void," Gen. 1:2) — to the pre-creation state where water covered everything and there was no dry ground. The narrative logic is: humanity's corruption has reversed the goodness of creation; YHWH uncreates and then re-creates, with Noah as the second Adam and the ark as the vessel of continuing life.

**The same cosmological structure recurs in:**
- Job 38:8-11 (YHWH "shut in the sea with doors" at creation)
- Ps. 104:6-9 (the waters "stood above the mountains" at creation and fled at YHWH's rebuke)
- Isa. 54:9 (YHWH swears "as in the days of Noah")
- 2 Pet. 3:5-6 (the world was "formed out of water and through water... and was deluged")

**DSS:** The Genesis Apocryphon (1QapGen) and the Book of Jubilees both preserve and amplify the cosmic-water theology of the flood. Jubilees 5:24 describes the "windows of heaven" and "fountains of the deep" with evident awareness of the cosmological significance of 7:11.

**LXX 7:11:** ἐρράγησαν πᾶσαι αἱ πηγαὶ τῆς ἀβύσσου καὶ αἱ καταρράκται τοῦ οὐρανοῦ ἠνεῴχθησαν. The LXX renders תְּהוֹם as ἄβυσσος (abyssos, "abyss") — the standard LXX rendering of תְּהוֹם, carried into English as "abyss." The windows (אֲרֻבֹּת) become καταρράκται ("cataracts/sluicegates") — a hydraulic image of controlled release. The verb for the fountains is ἐρράγησαν ("burst/cracked/broke") — preserving the violence of נִבְקְעוּ.

---

### On 7:13 — "On This Very Day" (בְּעֶצֶם הַיּוֹם הַזֶּה)

The phrase בְּעֶצֶם הַיּוֹם הַזֶּה ("on this very bone of the day") is a solemn emphatic expression of exact coincidence. עֶצֶם means "bone" and in this idiomatic construction means "the very substance/essence" of the day — "on this selfsame day." The same phrase is used of:
- The day of the Exodus from Egypt: Ex. 12:17, 12:41, 12:51 — "on this very day" Israel left Egypt
- The day of Moses' death: Deut. 32:48

The phrase marks epochal moments in sacred history. The entry into the ark on the day the flood begins is placed in the same rhetorical category as the Exodus — a moment of total historical rupture when one era ends and another begins.

---

### On 7:14 — The Parade of Creatures

Verse 14 is the narrative's great zoological inventory of those preserved. The four-fold classification echoes Genesis 1:
- חַיָּה (living thing / wild animal) — Gen. 1:24-25
- בְּהֵמָה (beast / domesticated animal) — Gen. 1:24-25
- רֶמֶשׂ (creeping thing) — Gen. 1:24-25, 1:30
- עוֹף (flying-thing) — Gen. 1:20-22

The creation taxonomy is preserved in the ark. The flood does not unmake the created order of kinds — it preserves it. The ark is the vessel of taxonomy itself: לְמִינָהּ / לְמִינֵהוּ ("according to its kind") echoes the Genesis 1 refrain (Gen. 1:11, 12, 21, 24, 25).

The final two terms — כֹּל צִפּוֹר כָּל-כָּנָף ("every bird, every winged-thing") — further sub-classify the עוֹף: צִפּוֹר is the standard word for bird; כָּנָף (lit. "wing") is used as a collective for all winged creatures. This is taxonomic completeness.

---

### On 7:16 — The Subject Change: Elohim to YHWH

וַיִּסְגֹּר יְהוָה בַּעֲדוֹ.
"And YHWH shut behind him."

This is one of the most theologically charged subject-changes in the Torah. The embarkation has been described in Elohim language throughout vv. 9-16a: כַּאֲשֶׁר צִוָּה אֱלֹהִים אֶת-נֹחַ ("as God commanded Noah," v. 9) and כַּאֲשֶׁר צִוָּה אֹתוֹ אֱלֹהִים ("as God commanded him," v. 16a). The procession into the ark — the cosmic, ordered, taxonomic entrance — is Elohim's domain.

But the closing of the door is YHWH's act.

The door of the ark was not shut by Noah. It was not shut by the animals. It was shut by YHWH himself. The preposition בַּעֲדוֹ ("behind him / on his behalf") is both spatial and relational: YHWH shuts the door behind Noah, sealing him in, sealing the world out. The personal name of the relational God performs the final act of protection and closure.

This is a moment of pastoral theology embedded in de-creation cosmology. The fountains of the deep are bursting (v. 11); the windows of heaven are open; the waters of chaos are released — and YHWH personally closes the door behind the one righteous man. The cosmic terror is not abandoned by the personal God.

The verb סָגַר (sagar, "to shut, close, deliver up") in the Qal means simply "to shut." The door is closed. The ark is sealed. The world is separated from the preserved remnant by a door that the divine hand has shut.

**Textual note:** Classical source criticism (Wellhausen) identifies this clause as a J interpolation into a P narrative, the subject change being a seam revealing the composite nature of the flood account. EMET notes this observation without adjudicating it: whatever its redactional history, the text in its final canonical form deploys the divine name change with unmistakable theological effect.

**LXX 7:16:** καὶ ἔκλεισεν ὁ θεὸς τὴν κιβωτὸν ἔξωθεν αὐτοῦ — "and God shut the ark outside him/from outside." The LXX reads θεός (God, = Elohim) rather than κύριος (Lord, = YHWH), eliminating the subject change and with it the distinctive theological force of the MT. This is a significant leveling: the LXX harmonizes to Elohim throughout, removing the name-change that in the MT is so pregnant with meaning.

---

### On 7:17-18 — The Ark Lifted: Three Verbs of Rising

Three verbs in rapid succession describe the ark's lifting in v. 17:
1. וַיִּרְבּוּ הַמַּיִם — "the waters multiplied/increased"
2. וַיִּשְׂאוּ אֶת-הַתֵּבָה — "and they lifted the ark" (נָשָׂא: to carry, lift, bear)
3. וַתָּרָם מֵעַל הָאָרֶץ — "and it was raised/exalted above the earth" (רוּם: to be high, exalted)

The verb רוּם ("to be raised/exalted") is the word used of divine exaltation (Ps. 99:2: יְהוָה רָם — "YHWH is exalted"). The ark is raised above the dying earth with vocabulary of elevation and exaltation. In the de-creation of everything else, the ark is lifted up. The remnant is not just preserved — it is elevated.

---

### On 7:19-20 — Universal Coverage and the Fifteen Cubits

Two verses establish the totality of the flood in ascending intensity:

**7:19:** וְהַמַּיִם גָּבְרוּ מְאֹד מְאֹד — "the waters prevailed exceedingly exceedingly." The doubling of מְאֹד ("very/exceedingly") is the superlative of intensification: beyond very, beyond very very. Then: all the high mountains under all the heavens were covered — כָּל-הֶהָרִים הַגְּבֹהִים אֲשֶׁר-תַּחַת כָּל-הַשָּׁמָיִם. The scope is double-universal: every mountain, under every heaven. No elevation is exempt.

**7:20:** חֲמֵשׁ עֶשְׂרֵה אַמָּה מִלְמַעְלָה גָּבְרוּ הַמַּיִם — "Fifteen cubits upward the waters prevailed." Fifteen cubits (approximately 22-23 feet / 7 meters) above the peaks of the highest mountains. This is not merely cosmetic coverage — it is submersion. The world is entirely under water.

The precision of "fifteen cubits" is notable. Some interpreters connect this to the ark's draft: if the ark drew approximately 15 cubits of water (half its 30-cubit height), then the statement that the waters rose 15 cubits above the mountains means the ark could float even over the highest peaks — the flood was exactly deep enough to float the ark over the mountains. Whether this arithmetic is deliberate or incidental, the text achieves total cosmic coverage.

**LXX 7:20:** πεντεκαίδεκα πήχεις ἐπάνω ὑψώθη τὸ ὕδωρ — "fifteen cubits above, the water was raised/exalted." The LXX uses ὑψώθη ("was exalted/raised high") for גָּבְרוּ ("prevailed") — a shift from the language of strength/prevailing to height/exaltation.

---

### On 7:21-22 — The Extinction Inventory

The death list of vv. 21-22 is the inverse of the life-preservation list of vv. 14-15. The same four categories perish that were preserved:
- עוֹף (flying-thing)
- בְּהֵמָה (beast)
- חַיָּה (living thing)
- שֶׁרֶץ (swarming/creeping thing)
- הָאָדָם (the adam)

The adam is listed last — the climactic extinction. The entire created order of Genesis 1 is systematically undone.

**7:22 — The Breath Formula:** כֹּל אֲשֶׁר נִשְׁמַת-רוּחַ חַיִּים בְּאַפָּיו — "all in whose nostrils was the breath of the spirit of life." This is the most elaborate breath-formula in the flood narrative. It combines:
- נִשְׁמַת (nishmat, "breath") — the word of Gen. 2:7, when YHWH breathed נִשְׁמַת חַיִּים into the adam's nostrils
- רוּחַ (ruach, "spirit/wind/breath") — the spirit that hovered over the waters in Gen. 1:2
- חַיִּים ("life")
- בְּאַפָּיו ("in his nostrils") — the exact location of the breath in Gen. 2:7

Everything that bears the life-breath of Genesis 2 — the breath that made the adam a living soul — dies. The creative act is reversed. The animating breath returns (implicitly) to its source.

**Limitation to dry-land creatures:** The verse specifies מִכֹּל אֲשֶׁר בֶּחָרָבָה — "from all that was on the dry land." Fish and sea creatures are not mentioned. The flood destroys land life; sea life continues. This is consistent with the cosmology: the flood is the return to the state of Gen. 1:2 (waters over everything), which is the natural state of the sea creatures, not their destruction.

---

### On 7:23 — The Wipe and the Remnant

וַיִּמַח אֶת-כָּל-הַיְקוּם — "And he wiped away every standing-thing." The subject is ambiguous — the Hebrew verb is third-person singular masculine but the subject is not explicitly stated. God? The waters? The ambiguity may be deliberate: the judgment is both divine act and natural catastrophe; the waters wipe as YHWH wipes.

The verb מָחָה ("to wipe, blot out") was promised in 6:7 (אֶמְחֶה) and repeated as a future in 7:4 (וּמָחִיתִי). Now it is accomplished: וַיִּמַח. The promise is kept. The divine word is fulfilled exactly.

The wipe goes from adam to beast to creeping thing to flying-thing — the same reverse-creation sequence of 6:7. Creation is unwound from its apex back toward its components.

וַיִּשָּׁאֶר אַךְ-נֹחַ — "And there remained only Noah." The word אַךְ ("only, but, yet") is a particle of restriction: Noah alone. The ark's passengers are included in his remainder (וַאֲשֶׁר אִתּוֹ בַּתֵּבָה — "and those who were with him in the ark"), but grammatically Noah is the singular survivor. He is the remnant — the שְׁאֵרִית, the surviving remnant that the prophets will later use as the vehicle of Israel's hope (Isa. 10:20-22; Mic. 5:7; Zeph. 3:13). The flood narrative is the prototype of the remnant theology.

---

### On 7:24 — One Hundred and Fifty Days

The waters prevailed 150 days. This corresponds to the five months between the 17th day of the 2nd month (7:11) and the 17th day of the 7th month (8:4), when the ark rested on Ararat. If the months are 30 days each: 5 × 30 = 150 days exactly. The flood narrative uses a 30-day month calendar (likely the ancient Near Eastern administrative calendar), and its arithmetic is precise and consistent.

The 150 days of prevailing waters is the zenith of the de-creation — the maximum extent of the flood before the turn of 8:1: וַיִּזְכֹּר אֱלֹהִים אֶת-נֹחַ ("And God remembered Noah"). The 150 days of death end with divine remembrance. Memory is the beginning of re-creation.

---

## LXX Comparison Summary for Genesis 7

| Verse | MT (Leningrad Codex) | LXX (Vaticanus) | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 7:1 | לְפָנַי ("before me") | ἐναντίον μου ("before/facing me") | LXX preserves the "before me" idiom correctly |
| 7:2 | אִישׁ וְאִשְׁתּוֹ ("a man and his woman") | ἄρσεν καὶ θῆλυ ("male and female") | LXX loses the relational/intimate pairing vocabulary |
| 7:11 | תְּהוֹם רַבָּה ("the great deep") | ἄβυσσος ("abyss") | LXX "abyss" → English "abyss" |
| 7:11 | אֲרֻבֹּת הַשָּׁמַיִם ("windows of the heavens") | καταρράκται τοῦ οὐρανοῦ ("cataracts/sluicegates of heaven") | LXX uses hydraulic engineering image |
| 7:16 | וַיִּסְגֹּר יְהוָה ("and YHWH shut") | καὶ ἔκλεισεν ὁ θεὸς ("and God shut") | LXX erases the YHWH/Elohim subject change — major theological difference |
| 7:19 | גָּבְרוּ מְאֹד מְאֹד ("prevailed exceedingly exceedingly") | ὑπερεπλεόνασεν ("super-abounded/overflowed") | LXX compresses the Hebrew double-intensive into a single compound verb |
| 7:20 | גָּבְרוּ הַמַּיִם ("the waters prevailed") | ὑψώθη τὸ ὕδωρ ("the water was raised") | LXX shifts from strength vocabulary to height vocabulary |
| 7:22 | נִשְׁמַת-רוּחַ חַיִּים | πνοὴν ζωῆς ("breath of life") | LXX omits רוּחַ (spirit/wind) — simplifies the doubled breath-formula |
| 7:23 | וַיִּשָּׁאֶר אַךְ-נֹחַ ("there remained only Noah") | κατελείφθη μόνος Νωε ("Noah alone was left behind") | LXX uses καταλείπω ("left behind") — passive; MT has active "remained" with the restrictive particle אַךְ |
